diagnosisNodes.name: Oligodendroglioma mimics
diagnosisNodes.description: A variety of mimics of oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted may be encountered in the CNS. These include “pediatric-type” oligodendroglioma, dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor, central and extraventricular neurocytoma and clear cell ependymoma, as well as astrocytic neoplasms with minimal pleomorphism. As a rule, the combination of IDH mutations and 1p/19q codeletion is not a feature of these mimics.

diagnosisNodes.name: Oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted
diagnosisNodes.description: Oligodendroglioma with IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion represents a distinct integrated category in the updated WHO classification  Morphologic features in addition to the molecular features are required for the diagnosis at the present time.

diagnosisNodes.name: Anaplastic oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted
diagnosisNodes.description: Oligodendroglioma with IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion represents a distinct integrated category in the updated WHO classification. Morphologic features in addition to the molecular features are required for the diagnosis at the present time. The anaplastic variants have in addition brisk mitotic activity and/or microvascular proliferation and/or necrosis.

diagnoses.name: Anaplastic oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted

diagnosisNodes.name: Diffuse astrocytoma, IDH mutant
diagnosisNodes.description: Diffuse astrocytoma with IDH mutation represents a distinct subset in the new WHO classification. Morphologic features include cytologic atypia but low to absent mitotic activity. The presence of focal oligodendroglioma features (e.g. round cells with perinuclear halos) are still compatible with the diagnosis, particularly if the characteristic molecular features (e.g. lack of 1p/19q codeletion, ATRX loss) are present.

diagnosisNodes.name: Diffuse astrocytoma, IDH wild type
diagnosisNodes.description: Diffuse astrocytomas lacking IDH mutation are relatively rare in the adult population, but are considered distinct from IDH mutant counterparts. This category is probably heterogeneous at the molecular level.

diagnosisNodes.name: Anaplastic astrocytoma, IDH mutant
diagnosisNodes.description: Anaplastic astrocytoma, IDH mutant in addition to cytologic atypia demonstrates significant mitotic activity, greater than that required for a diagnosis of anaplasia in IDH wild type tumors.

diagnosisNodes.name: Anaplastic astrocytoma, IDH wild type
diagnosisNodes.description: The lack of IDH mutations in anaplastic astrocytomas is associated with a worse biology and prognosis. In some studies, the outcome is similar to glioblastoma. Although a specific mitotic cutoff has not been specified, more than one mitotic figure should be present, particularly in larger specimens.

diagnosisNodes.name: Glioblastoma, IDH mutant
diagnosisNodes.description: IDH mutations are associated with a better prognosis in glioblastoma, but the morphologic criteria are the same, i.e. a mitotically active infiltrating astrocytoma with necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation. IDH mutation is also the molecular correlate of the “secondary” glioblastoma, usually arising in younger patients, rogressing from a lower grade precursor.

diagnosisNodes.name: Glioblastoma, IDH wild type
diagnosisNodes.description: Glioblastoma, IDH wild type is defined as a mitotically activeinfiltrating astrocytoma with necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation, but lacking IDH mutations. This glioma subtype is associated in general with the worse prognosis among diffuse gliomas.

featureNodes.name: Oligodendroglial features
featureNodes.description: Oligodendroglial features include minimal cell variation, round nuclei and small nucleoli. Perinuclear cytoplasmic clearing imparts the colorful analogy to "fried eggs", but is not always present since it represents an artifact of delayed fixation.

diagnosisNodes.name: Anaplastic oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted

featureNodes.name: Astrocytic features
featureNodes.description: Astrocytic features include nuclear hyperchromasia, irregular shape, elongation, and inconspicuous nucleoli. Pleomorphism in astrocytic neoplasms contrasts with round cell monotony typical of oligodendrogliomas.

featureNodes.name: IDH wild type and 1p/19q intact
featureNodes.description: A variety of neoplasms share morphologic features with oligodendroglioma, including round nuclei and perinuclear halos, but generally lack 1p19q co-deletion and IDH mutations. These include the central neurocytoma, a known mimic of oligodendroglioma but with a predilection for intraventricular locations.

featureNodes.name: IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted
featureNodes.description: Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p19q codeleted is a distinct entity in the current WHO classification with important prognostic and therapeutic implications. Both molecular alterations are required for the diagnosis. IDH1 p.R132H mutation recognized by immunohistochemistry is illustrated in this example.

diagnosisNodes.name: Anaplastic oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted

featureNodes.name: Brisk mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, necrosis absent
featureNodes.description: Oligodendroglioma (low grade) in addition to monotonous round cells, lacks brisk mitotic activity, microvascular changes or spontaneous necrosis, in contrast to its anaplastic counterpart.

featureNodes.name: Brisk mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, necrosis present
featureNodes.description: Brisk mitotic activity and microvascular proliferation when present characterize anaplastic oligodendroglioma. Endothelial hypertrophy, short of overt microvascular proliferation, appears as “plump” capillaries and is also frequently encountered in anaplastic oligodendrogliomas.

diagnosisNodes.name: Anaplastic oligodendroglioma, IDH mutant and 1p/19q codeleted

featureNodes.name: Low/absent mitotic rate
featureNodes.description: Low or absent mitotic activity in an infiltrating astrocytoma characterizes diffuse astrocytoma (WHO grade II).

featureNodes.name: Increased mitotic rate
featureNodes.description: Evident mitotic activity, usually associated with increased cellularity, characterizes anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO grade III).

featureNodes.name: IDH mutation present
featureNodes.description: Immunohistochemistry recognizes the most frequent IDH1 mutations (p.R132H). Identification of IDH mutations in infiltrating gliomas is relevant since they characterize specific subsets of infiltrating gliomas representing distinct categories in the WHO classification.

featureNodes.name: IDH mutation absent
featureNodes.description: Infiltrating astrocytomas lacking IDH mutations are associated with a worse prognosis and a distinct biology from IDH mutant astrocytomas, and are separated in the current WHO classification.

featureNodes.name: Necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation present
featureNodes.description: Necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation in a mitotically active infiltrating astrocytoma is diagnostic for glioblastoma.

featureNodes.name: Necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation absent
featureNodes.description: Anaplastic astrocytomas demonstrate obvious mitotic activity but lack necrosis or microvascular proliferation, which separates them from glioblastoma.

featureNodes.name: IDH mutation present
featureNodes.description: Immunohistochemistry recognizes the most frequent IDH1 mutations (p.R132H). Identification of IDH mutations in infiltrating gliomas is relevant since they characterize specific subsets of infiltrating gliomas representing distinct categories in the WHO classification.

featureNodes.name: IDH mutation absent
featureNodes.description: Infiltrating astrocytomas lacking IDH mutations are associated with a worse prognosis and a distinct biology from IDH mutant astrocytomas, and are separated in the current WHO classification.

featureNodes.name: IDH mutation present
featureNodes.description: Immunohistochemistry recognizes the most frequent IDH1 mutations (p.R132H). Identification of IDH mutations in infiltrating gliomas is relevant since they characterize specific subsets of infiltrating gliomas representing distinct categories in the WHO classification.

featureNodes.name: IDH mutation absent
featureNodes.description: Infiltrating astrocytomas lacking IDH mutations are associated with a worse prognosis and a distinct biology from IDH mutant astrocytomas, and are separated in the current WHO classification.
